DEMURRER TO ANSWER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Mar-28-2017 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances in the matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. PLAINTIFF JANE DOE's DEMURRER TO ANSWER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT as to certain affirmative defenses filed by defendant Shirley Stiver is overruled as to all of the affirmative defenses that are the subject of the demurrer. This demurrer serves no useful purpose. While plaintiff is correct that some of the purported affirmative defenses are not truly affirmative defenses and others do not contain much, if any, factual allegations, all of the affirmative defenses conform to the longstanding custom and practice of answers in Bay Area state courts. Almost assuredly very few of the affirmative defenses will be asserted in either motion practice or at trial and thus most of them are plead "out of abundance" caution in the event that facts are discovered that support them. This "abundance of caution" pleading of affirmative defenses, while perhaps not laudable, has been accepted for a long time. If plaintiff truly wants to learn the factual bases for the affirmative defenses and seek their disposition, she can check the appropriate boxes on the form interrogatories and requests for admissions and, if no facts are provided, can seek a stipulation that the affirmative defenses are not viable or, failing receipt of such a stipulation, file a motion for summary adjudication. Prevailing party shall prepare form of order that complies with CRC 3.1312. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Felicia Green; Court Reporter: Carol Karen, CSR No. 8189, email: ckaren@sftc.org; Dept. 302.

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Dec-06-2016 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances: Brendan Gannon and Mark Burton, Jr. of Hersh and Hersh telephone number 415-441-5544 for Plaintiff Jane Doe.
Aaron Cargain and Tad Devlin of Kaufman Dolowich & Voluck LLP telephone number 415-926-7619 for Defendant Dr. Shirley Stiver.

The matter was argued. Upon conclusion of argument, the Court confirmed its tentative ruling.
Defendant Shirley Stiver's demurrer to the complaint is overruled in its entirety. Paragraph 22 of the complaint alleges that Dr. Stiver took the information and improperly disclosed it to an attorney. Paragraph 48 alleges that the HIV test was disclosed. Those allegations adequately support the first and second causes of action. Dr. Stiver fails to show that any of her defenses bar either of the two causes of action in their entirety. Civil Code 56.10(b)(3) only bars liability based on responding to a discovery request and plaintiff alleges more than that. Dr. Stiver fails to show that Civil Code 47 bars the claim. Paragraph 22 alleges that Dr. Stiver began removing documents in 2011 and she was fired in 2013. Paragraphs 20-23 allege that thousands of documents were taken and thousands of patients were affected. The court cannot find at this point that the records were connected to Dr. Stiver's litigation or achieved a litigation purpose. Dr. Stiver also does not conclusively show that Civil Code 56.10(c)(4), Civil Code 56.10(c)(14), or 45 CFR 164.502(j) allowed the disclosure. Those defenses/exemptions raise factual issues. As to the demurrer to the class allegations, the court cannot determine at this point solely based on the allegations in the complaint that individual issues predominate or that plaintiff is an inadequate representative. Order signed in open Court.

Judge: Harold Kahn. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976. (302/HK)

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Dec-06-2016 Sustained
MINI MINUTES:

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel or Interested Person.

The Court confirmed its tentative ruling. The Regents of the University of California's demurrer to both causes of action in the complaint is sustained with twenty days leave to amend as to both causes of action. The first cause of action for violation of Confidentiality of Medical Information Act (Civil Code 56 et seq.) fails to allege sufficient facts to state a viable claim. Sutter Health v. Superior Court (2014) 227 Cal.App.4th 1546 explains that a health care provider can be liable where it "discloses" confidential medical information. Civil Code sec. 56.10(a) covers "disclosures" and a disclosure occurs when there is an affirmative communicative act. Sutter Health also explains that liability can exist where a health care provider "releases" medical information. Civil Code 56.36 covers "releases" and that statute applies when a health care provider negligently maintains its records and allows unauthorized persons to gain access to the information. The bases for plaintiff's claims against the Regents are unclear. Paragraph 20 of the complaint suggests that plaintiff's first cause of action is based on Dr. Stiver's disclosure of medical information in response to a discovery request. CC 56.10(b)(3) protects such a disclosure. Paragraphs 21-22 indicate that plaintiff seeks to recover because Dr. Stiver disclosed the information to her attorney when pursuing her employment action. In opposition to the demurrer, however, plaintiff argues that the Regents is liable because it negligently allowed Dr. Stiver to access the information. Plaintiff argues that Dr. Stiver was unauthorized to view the records. (Plaintiff's Opposition, 3:6-7.) That theory of recovery fails in light of plaintiff's allegation in paragraph 18 of her complaint that Dr. Stiver was her treating physician. It is also unclear whether plaintiff is attempting to hold the Regents liable on a respondeat superior theory based on the disclosure or for its direct negligence based on the release. These same defects apply to the second cause of action per Health and Safety Code 120980. That statute bars the negligent or willful disclosure of an "HIV test, as defined in subdivision (c) of Section 120775." It is not clear whether plaintiff is attempting to impose liability on the Regents based upon a disclosure rather than a release. Order signed.

Judge: Harold Kahn. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976. (302/HK)
